note: Two dimensions: (A) the open/industry data standards NYC domains should conform to (and their current, mostly-zero adoption); (B) the standards this project itself is built on.

domainStandards.name: Open311 (GeoReport v2)
domainStandards.what: An open API standard for submitting and tracking non-emergency service requests / complaints.
domainStandards.why: The exact shape of a 311-style report. NYC pioneered it, then let it lapse — reviving it standardizes every complaint/inspection intake across agencies.

domainStandards.name: HL7 FHIR / SMART on FHIR
domainStandards.what: The healthcare interoperability standard for clinical and administrative resources (Patient, Appointment, Location…).
domainStandards.why: H+H already runs a live FHIR endpoint; extending it (booking, an open facility directory) is standards-native modernization, not a bespoke build.

domainStandards.name: OpenReferral / HSDS
domainStandards.what: The Human Services Data Specification — a standard for directories of community services, programs, and eligibility.
domainStandards.why: Every human-services agency publishes a bespoke provider directory; HSDS would make them queryable and interoperable (and feed 211/ACCESS NYC).

domainStandards.name: Open Contracting (OCDS)
domainStandards.what: The Open Contracting Data Standard for planning, tender, award, contract, and spending records.
domainStandards.why: Checkbook NYC is a custom XML feed; procurement is scattered across PASSPort, City Record, and agency portals. OCDS would unify the money.

domainStandards.name: GTFS / GTFS-realtime
domainStandards.what: The General Transit Feed Specification for schedules, stops, and real-time vehicle positions.
domainStandards.why: NYC Ferry and DOT mobility services could publish GTFS so trip-planners and agents consume them natively.

domainStandards.name: iCalendar (RFC 5545)
domainStandards.what: The interoperable calendar standard for events and schedules.
domainStandards.why: School calendars, park events, council hearings, and library programs are HTML today; iCalendar makes them subscribable and agent-readable.

domainStandards.name: Popolo / Open Civic Data
domainStandards.what: A standard for legislatures, people, organizations, memberships, and votes.
domainStandards.why: Council runs Legistar (structured but vendor); the borough presidents and DAs model people/offices ad hoc. Popolo standardizes who-represents-whom.

projectStandards.name: JSON Schema (2020-12)
projectStandards.what: A vocabulary for describing and validating the shape of a JSON object.
projectStandards.why: The atom of the whole design — one canonical, machine-validatable schema per entity (Park, Permit, School…). `$ref` lets schemas reuse shared definitions, which is exactly what makes a citywide `nyc-commons` possible.

projectStandards.name: OpenAPI 3.1
projectStandards.what: A standard, language-agnostic description of a REST API — its paths, operations, and the schemas they read and write.
projectStandards.why: Turns a pile of schemas into a described, resource-oriented API. It `$ref`s the JSON Schemas and drives docs, mocks, SDKs, and validation — the lingua franca every API tool speaks.

projectStandards.name: OpenAPI Overlays
projectStandards.what: A separate document of targeted updates (JSONPath actions) applied to a base OpenAPI description, without forking or editing the base.
projectStandards.why: The transformation layer. One base contract + N thin overlays = N variants — used here to localize the INTERFACE (titles, summaries, descriptions) into the ten citywide languages of NYC Local Law 30, never touching paths, operationIds, schema fields, or data. Because MCP tools derive 1:1 from OpenAPI operations, the same overlay yields a localized MCP server. 10 language overlays for the HPD worked example — see experience/overlays.

projectStandards.name: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
projectStandards.what: An open protocol for exposing tools and resources to AI agents/assistants.
projectStandards.why: The agent-native layer. It maps the same resources as callable tools, mapped 1:1 to the OpenAPI operations — making a government service usable by an AI agent, not just a browser. Zero of 70 domains have this today.

projectStandards.name: Agent Skills
projectStandards.what: Portable, model-agnostic skill definitions that package a task's instructions, the resources it needs, and the tools it orchestrates for an AI agent.
projectStandards.why: The layer above MCP tools: one skill per common government process (apply, report, request records, schedule, pay…) that resolves the right agency and drives its tools to finish a citizen task. Ten are defined across the 70 agencies — see the Programmable City experience layer.

projectStandards.name: Arazzo 1.0
projectStandards.what: The OpenAPI Initiative's standard for describing a sequence of API calls across one or more APIs — a portable, versionable, testable description of a multi-step process.
projectStandards.why: The cross-agency layer: a single government outcome (build affordable housing, open a business) rarely lives in one agency. Arazzo chains the per-agency OpenAPI operations into one machine-readable journey — the connective tissue for automating approval management across agencies, as an open standard rather than another siloed portal.

projectStandards.name: APIs.json
projectStandards.what: A machine-readable index that catalogs an organization's APIs and their supporting artifacts (schemas, OpenAPI, docs).
projectStandards.why: The connective tissue open data never had — a discoverable registry so humans and agents can find every agency's API. The planned citywide index (see roadmap).

projectStandards.name: Socrata SODA / SoQL
projectStandards.what: The query API + query language behind NYC Open Data (data.cityofnewyork.us).
projectStandards.why: The existing citywide data layer (run by OTI) that this project crosswalks every domain against — the 2,681 assets we mapped and the read-side many proposed APIs would wrap.
